topic: Someone finds out they have a half-sibling they never knew about
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope was tucked beneath a stack of old tax documents, mundane until the seal broke. My fingers trembled as I slit the brittle paper open. The handwriting was elegant, unfamiliar, yet there was a familiar slant to the script that snagged in my memory. Inside, a single name: Elias Vance. My breath hitched, a sharp, involuntary intake of air that seemed to steal the oxygen from the room. I reread the letter three times, my eyes blurring over the neat, formal paragraphs detailing a shared, unknown lineage. A coldness washed over my skin, settling deep in my chest. I dropped the paper; it fluttered to the worn carpet, unnoticed. My hands were slick with sweat, and I couldn't seem to form a coherent sound, just a low, ragged exhale escaping my lips. The world seemed to tilt, the familiar shapes of the room warping into something alien and impossibly vast.

[story 2]

I was organizing the attic, a chore I’d been dreading for months, when I stumbled upon the box. It wasn't labeled. When I lifted the lid, the contents spilled out—old photographs, faded letters, and a single, official-looking birth certificate. My vision tunneled. The name on the document, Elias Vance, was printed in stark black ink. My heart began hammering against my ribs, a frantic drumbeat echoing in the silence. I stared at the paper, my knuckles white where I gripped the edge of the trunk. A dizzying sensation started in my stomach, a churning nausea that made me lean against a dusty wardrobe for support. I let out a small, choked sound, a sound more like a gasp than a word, and I quickly flipped the page, desperate to confirm what my eyes were betraying.

[story 3]

It happened during a routine genealogical deep dive. I was cross-referencing old family trees on my laptop, tracing lines I thought were solid, when a name popped up that didn't belong. A branch, entirely separate, connected directly to my own. My fingers froze mid-scroll, the screen reflecting a sudden, blinding glare in my wide eyes. I physically recoiled from the desk, pushing my chair back with a screech that sounded deafening in the quiet house. My mouth went dry; I tried to swallow, but nothing came down. A strange, hollow feeling expanded through my chest, pressing against my lungs, making each inhalation feel labored and shallow. I stood there, rooted to the spot, unable to move, simply watching the screen as if it were a foreign, terrifying landscape.
